blocks.text: The National Eligibility cum Entrance Test, known as NEET, is India's single largest gateway to undergraduate medical education. Millions of students sit the exam each year for a comparatively small number of seats, and results carry enormous weight for family expectations, career prospects and years of preparation. Because so much rests on one test, any doubt about its integrity resonates far beyond the students who take it.

In May 2026, authorities canceled NEET results and ordered a re-examination after allegations that question papers had leaked before the test. The reversal affected candidates who believed they had already secured their places, and reports of at least 13 student suicides in the surrounding weeks intensified public anger. Coverage framed the episode as the latest in a series of examination controversies that had eroded trust in the system.
How the Cockroach Janta Party formed

The movement began online as political satire. Its name references a remark attributed to a Chief Justice that compared unemployed youth to cockroaches, a phrase protesters reclaimed as an ironic badge of identity. What started as memes and posts spread quickly across social media and hardened into an organized campaign, with the label Cockroach Janta Party used as a rallying identity rather than a formal registered party.

Protesters have centered their demands on the resignation of the education minister and on structural reform of the national examination system, including stronger safeguards against paper leaks and clearer accountability when results are challenged. Supporters describe these as practical fixes rather than partisan goals, and the campaign has drawn participants from across political affiliations.

After weeks of permitted demonstrations at Jantar Mantar, a change in Delhi Police leadership preceded the clearing of the protest camp in mid-July and the forcible removal of a hunger-striking activist to a hospital. When more than 10,000 people marched toward Parliament on July 20, police used tear gas and batons, with around 180 reported injured and 70 detained. Organizers then paused street marches over safety concerns while vowing to continue the protest.

The unrest sits against a backdrop of high graduate youth unemployment, reported above 15 percent, which has left many educated young people frustrated by limited opportunities. For many participants the exam scandal became a symbol of wider grievances about fairness, mobility and the gap between qualifications earned and jobs available.
